field_visibility.convention: Any key beginning with underscore (_) is metadata-only. `slow_input.explanation` is kept in catalog as audit/documentation but stripped before LLM injection (it inflates prompt without adding decision-relevant info beyond construction + complexity).

policy.complexity_notation: ASCII only. Use n^2, n*log(n), n*log(n)^2, n^(3/2), n^(4/3), 2^n, sqrt(n), alpha(n). Do not use Greek letters or unicode math symbols.

vulnerability_classes.asymptotic: Adversarial input changes the asymptotic order (e.g., n*log(n) -> n^2).
vulnerability_classes.constant_factor: Asymptotic order is preserved by the implementation, but a specific input pattern maximizes the constant factor (typically 2x-3x more comparisons or operations).
vulnerability_classes.size_only: No input pattern materially affects runtime; only raising n to its constraint upper bound stresses the implementation. Catalog entry exists for completeness and routing safety (prevents misapplication of unrelated patterns).
vulnerability_classes.randomized: Implementation uses randomization. Asymptotic adversary requires knowledge of the random seed; without seed access the implementation is effectively safe.
vulnerability_classes.variant_dependent: Worst-case behavior depends on a parameter of the implementation (e.g., gap sequence in shell sort). Multiple variants must be enumerated.

sorting.scenario_summary: Problem requires producing a sorted permutation of an input sequence (or a derived quantity computed via sorting).

slow_input.construction: Strictly decreasing sequence: a[i] = (n - i) for i in 0..n-1, optionally shifted by lo.
Strictly decreasing sequence: a[i] = (n - i) for i in 0..n-1.
slow_input.best_complexity: n (already-sorted input; only n-1 comparisons, no shifts)

slow_input.construction: No input-pattern adversary exists. Use n at the constraint upper bound; data values can be arbitrary (any permutation, all-equal, sorted, reverse-sorted all yield identical comparison counts).
slow_input.worst_complexity: n^2 (always, input-invariant at the comparison level)

slow_input.construction: Globally alternating high/low pattern: a = [1, n, 2, n-1, 3, n-2, ...] of length n. For natural merge sort variants that detect existing runs, the same alternating pattern is even more effective (run length is 1 everywhere).
slow_input.best_complexity: n*log(n) (smaller constant), or n for natural merge sort on already-sorted input

slow_input.construction: Already-sorted ascending sequence a[i] = i+1, or reverse-sorted, depending on whether the pivot is the first or last element of the partition.
slow_input.best_complexity: n*log(n) (balanced partitions, e.g., uniformly random data)

slow_input.construction: Bentley-McIlroy adversarial sequence — an interactive construction in which the antagonist defers value assignment until the algorithm queries a position, then assigns values so that the median of the three sampled positions is always the smallest or largest available element. A reference C implementation by McIlroy is publicly available as aqsort.c.

If the random seed is known to the adversary, simulate the algorithm's pivot choices and craft a sequence forcing pivot extremity at every level — analogous to the median-of-3 attack but seed-specific. If the seed is not known, no deterministic adversarial input exists.
slow_input.worst_complexity: n^2 (probabilistic; effective only with seed knowledge)

slow_input.construction: Descending sorted sequence: a[i] = (n - i) for i in 0..n-1. After the build-heap phase, repeated extract-max forces sift-down to traverse nearly to a leaf on every iteration.

Worst-case input depends on the gap sequence chosen by the implementation. For Shell's original 2^k-style gaps (n/2, n/4, ..., 1), an interleaved descending pattern resists each gap level until the final gap-1 insertion-sort pass, giving n^2 runtime. For Hibbard's (2^k - 1) gaps the worst case is n^(3/2); for Sedgewick's careful gaps it is n^(4/3); for Pratt's 3-smooth (2^a * 3^b) sequence it is n*log(n)^2 with no known asymptotic adversary.
slow_input.worst_complexity: n^2 (Shell's original gaps); n^(3/2) (Hibbard); n^(4/3) (Sedgewick); n*log(n)^2 (Pratt)

hashing.scenario_summary: Problem uses or implies a hash table (set/map/dict) for membership checks, frequency counts, or de-duplication; OR uses polynomial rolling hash for string operations.

implementations.name: Hash table with identity hash for integer keys (no defense)

slow_input.construction: Insert keys that are multiples of a known modulus used by the hash table's bucket array (e.g., 107897, 126271 for libstdc++ std::unordered_map prime sizes). All keys collide into a single bucket; the chain grows linearly.

implementations.name: Hash table with chained collision resolution, no fallback (pre-Java 8 / unprotected)

slow_input.construction: Insert n strings crafted to share a common hashCode value. Classic example for Java String: 'Aa' and 'BB' both hash to 2112; chain a sequence of such pairs. All n strings map to one bucket, chain length n.

implementations.name: Hash table with chaining + tree fallback (Java 8+ HashMap)

slow_input.construction: Same collision-string construction as the unprotected variant; the bucket converts to a red-black tree once it exceeds 8 entries (TREEIFY_THRESHOLD).

implementations.name: Hash table with SipHash + per-process random seed (Python dict, Ruby Hash)

slow_input.construction: If PYTHONHASHSEED is fixed (env var set, debug mode, or pre-3.4): collision sequences specific to that seed can be precomputed. With default randomized seed: no deterministic adversarial input.

implementations.name: Polynomial rolling hash with a single modulus

slow_input.construction: Construct strings with crafted collisions under (base, mod). For mod ~ 10^9 the birthday bound is sqrt(mod) ~ 3*10^4 trial strings to find a collision pair. Stage many such collision pairs as test input; the algorithm's hash-based comparison succeeds spuriously, forcing fallback to character-by-character comparison.
slow_input.worst_complexity: n^2 (when fallback verification is naive comparison)

implementations.name: Polynomial rolling hash with two independent moduli

slow_input.construction: Birthday attack on two independent moduli p1, p2 ~ 10^9 each requires sqrt(p1*p2) ~ 10^9 trial strings — computationally infeasible during contest time. No practical adversarial input exists.

tree_traversal.scenario_summary: Problem requires traversing a tree (DFS / BFS / LCA / centroid decomposition) or computing tree DP over n vertices. Tree given as n-1 edges or as parent array.

slow_input.construction: Bamboo tree (chain): edges = [(i, i+1) for i in 1..n-1]. Tree depth = n-1.
No input-pattern adversary. Use n at constraint upper bound; tree shape is irrelevant for asymptotic work.
Star tree: edges = [(1, i) for i in 2..n], where vertex 1 is the center connected to all others.

implementations.name: Lowest common ancestor by naive parent walking

slow_input.construction: Bamboo tree + queries (u, v) where u is the deepest leaf and v is its sibling near the root, OR queries between two leaves on opposite ends of a long path. Each query walks the full tree depth.

implementations.name: Tree DP via single post-order traversal

slow_input.construction: No input-pattern adversary. Use n at constraint upper bound.

graph_shortest_path.scenario_summary: Problem requires shortest paths on a graph (single source, all-pairs, with/without negative weights, with queries).

implementations.name: Dijkstra with lazy decrease-key on a priority queue (push duplicates)

slow_input.construction: Dense graph (E ~ V^2) with edge weights chosen so that many vertices receive updates from multiple paths. Each update pushes a new entry to the priority queue without removing the old one.
No input-pattern adversary. Use V, E at constraint upper bounds.
No input-pattern adversary for the basic algorithm. Use V, E at constraint upper bounds.
No input-pattern adversary. Use V at constraint upper bound.
No input-pattern adversary. Use V, E at constraint upper bounds.

Adversarial graphs documented in 'SPFA died' literature: long chains with strategic negative-weight back-edges that force every vertex to be re-relaxed many times. Specific constructions are published in Codeforces blogs.

string_matching.scenario_summary: Problem requires substring search, palindrome detection, suffix structure, or pattern matching on a string of length n.

slow_input.construction: Text = 'a'*(n-1) + 'b'; pattern = 'a'*(m-1) + 'b'. Each shift compares m-1 characters before mismatching at the last; n shifts total give Theta(n*m).
No input-pattern adversary. Use n, m at constraint upper bounds.

Same construction as polynomial_rolling_hash_single_mod: birthday-paradox collision pairs under (base, mod). With single mod ~10^9, sqrt(mod) ~ 3*10^4 trial strings find collisions. Crafted text and pattern produce many false-positive hash matches.

implementations.name: Suffix array via comparison-based sort of suffixes

slow_input.construction: Highly repetitive string, e.g., 'ab'*(n/2) (period 2). Suffix comparisons share long common prefixes, forcing each comparison to scan many characters before differing.
No input-pattern adversary. Use string length at constraint upper bound.

bst_data_structure.scenario_summary: Problem uses a binary search tree, balanced search tree, or related order-statistic data structure (segment tree, Fenwick tree).

slow_input.construction: Insert keys in sorted order (1, 2, 3, ..., n) or reverse-sorted. The BST degenerates into a linked list; recursion depth = n.
No input-pattern adversary. Use n at constraint upper bound.
No input-pattern adversary that breaks amortized bound. Sequential or sorted access produces the slowest individual operations but the amortized bound is preserved.
slow_input.worst_complexity: log(n) amortized (n worst single op, balanced over m ops)

slow_input.construction: If the random seed used for priority assignment is known, construct an insertion sequence such that all priorities form a sorted order, degenerating the treap to a linked list. Without seed knowledge, no deterministic adversary.
No input-pattern adversary. Use n, q at constraint upper bounds.

dp_recursion.scenario_summary: Problem requires dynamic programming or recursive computation over states.

slow_input.construction: Use n at constraint upper bound; the algorithm itself has exponential blowup on any non-trivial input. For Fibonacci-like recursion (two recursive calls per state), input n=40 is already untenable.
No input-pattern adversary beyond raising n. Total states is determined by problem structure.
No input-pattern adversary. Use n at constraint upper bound.

number_theory.scenario_summary: Problem involves number-theoretic computation: GCD/LCM, prime sieve, factorization, modular arithmetic.

slow_input.construction: Pair of consecutive Fibonacci numbers (F[k], F[k+1]) — these maximize the number of division steps for a given input size, by Lame's theorem.
No input-pattern adversary. Use n at constraint upper bound.
Set N to be a large prime (or a product of two primes near sqrt(N_max)) so that trial division must check all candidates up to sqrt(N).
slow_input.worst_complexity: sqrt(N) (when N has no small factors)

binary_search.scenario_summary: Problem uses binary search either as a primitive (lower_bound / upper_bound on a sorted array) or as a meta-strategy (binary search on the answer over a monotone predicate). Adversarial inputs aim to force the maximum number of predicate evaluations / log(range) iterations under the most expensive per-evaluation work.

implementations.name: Binary search on the answer with the answer at the extreme of the search range

slow_input.construction: Pick the search range [lo, hi] at its full constraint extent (e.g., hi - lo = 10^9 or 10^18). Construct the input so the feasibility predicate flips only at the largest valid value, forcing the binary search to run the full log2(hi-lo) iterations AND each predicate evaluation must scan the entire input.

implementations.name: lower_bound on an array with all-equal elements (forces full equal-range traversal in naive impl)

slow_input.construction: All n elements equal to a single value v. Query lower_bound(v) and upper_bound(v) repeatedly (q queries, q = 10^5).

two_pointers.scenario_summary: Problem uses a two-pointer / sliding-window scan over a sequence. The window grows or shrinks based on a condition; total work is amortized O(n) when each pointer advances monotonically. Adversarial inputs target implementations that re-scan the window or fail to amortize.

implementations.name: Naive sliding window that re-scans on every shrink (O(n^2))

slow_input.construction: Pick n at its constraint maximum. Construct an array where the window-validity condition becomes invalid for nearly every position pair, forcing a fresh inner loop. Example: an array where each element triggers the condition individually but the two-pointer right pointer must reset on every left advance (e.g., a permutation arranged so distinct-element counts spike alternately).

bitmasks.scenario_summary: Problem uses bitmask DP, subset enumeration, or sum-over-subsets DP over a small set of n elements (typically n ≤ 20-25). Worst-case is driven by 2^n state space; SOS DP and TSP-style transitions can reach 2^n*n or even 3^n if implemented naively (iterating submasks).

implementations.name: Held-Karp / bitmask DP forcing the full 2^n state space at n_max

slow_input.construction: Pin n to its constraint maximum (typically n=20 or n=22). Construct the input so every (mask, last) state is reachable and the transition cost cannot be pruned (e.g., a TSP graph with all positive distinct edges, or a covering-set instance where every element lies in many sets, defeating set-pruning).
slow_input.best_complexity: n^2 * 2^n (no prune possible at this size)

implementations.name: Sum-over-subsets DP via naive submask iteration (O(3^n))

slow_input.construction: Pin n to constraint max (n=20). Provide values f[mask] that are all non-zero, so the implementation cannot short-circuit on zero entries. The naive SOS impl iterates every submask of every mask: ∑_{m=0..2^n-1} 2^popcount(m) = 3^n.

combinatorics.scenario_summary: Problem requires counting configurations (permutations, combinations, ways), typically under a modulus. Worst-case for naive implementations comes from large n forcing factorial / binomial computation, or deep inclusion-exclusion enumeration over many constraints.

implementations.name: n!, C(n,k), inv factorials computed without precomputation

slow_input.construction: Pin n to constraint max (n = 10^6). Issue q queries (q = 10^5) for distinct C(n_i, k_i) values that span the full [0..n] range so memoization caches don't help.

implementations.name: Inclusion-exclusion over k constraints with k near 20+

slow_input.construction: k constraints (forbidden patterns / required-property checks) with k = 20-25. Each combined predicate among 2^k subsets is non-trivial to compute. Construct the input so every subset evaluates to a non-zero contribution (no early termination).

geometry.scenario_summary: Problem operates on geometric primitives: points, line segments, polygons, circles, and asks for properties such as intersection, area, distance, or hull. Adversaries target degenerate configurations (collinear points, coincident segments, all points on a circle) which break implementations that assume general position.

implementations.name: Convex hull on a degenerate set (all points collinear, n at max)

slow_input.construction: n = 10^5 points, all lying on the line y = x (or any single line). Coordinates spread uniformly so duplicates are rare. Naive Graham-scan / Andrew's monotone-chain handles this in O(n*log n), but implementations that perform extra checks for collinearity (e.g., via three-way orientation) revisit each point multiple times.

implementations.name: Closest pair of points on a regular grid layout (kd-tree degenerates)

slow_input.construction: n points placed at (i*1, j*1) for i,j in [0..sqrt(n)]. All pairwise distances of identical magnitude, so kd-tree pruning does not skip subtrees.
